enrichment.short_description: Open Web Analytics is a free, open-source, self-hosted PHP web analytics platform with heatmaps, session recording, and e-commerce tracking.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Open Web Analytics (OWA): free, open-source, self-hosted PHP analytics with heatmaps, session recording, and e-commerce tracking. GPL-2.0.

features.title: Pageview & visitor tracking
features.description: Tracks visits, pageviews, and e-commerce transactions on unlimited domains from a single self-hosted instance.

features.title: Heatmaps & session recording
features.description: Generates click heatmaps and visual session replays (Domstream) to show how visitors interact with pages.

features.title: Multi-site management
features.description: Monitor and report on multiple websites from one OWA installation.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Programmatic access to tracking data and administration for custom integrations.

features.title: Visitor geolocation
features.description: Reports visitor location data alongside standard traffic metrics.

features.title: Extensible module framework
features.description: Add custom functionality through OWA's pluggable module system.

pros: Free and open source under GPL-2.0, no licensing fees
Full data ownership via self-hosting
Includes heatmaps and session recording, uncommon in free analytics tools
REST API enables custom reporting and integrations
Established GitHub project with 2.7k+ stars
cons: Requires self-hosting and maintaining a PHP/MySQL server stack
No official managed or cloud-hosted version documented
UI and documentation are less polished than commercial analytics tools
Smaller community and support ecosystem than mainstream analytics platforms

faqs.question: Is Open Web Analytics free?
faqs.answer: Yes, OWA is free and open source under the GPL-2.0 license.

faqs.question: Do I need to self-host OWA?
faqs.answer: Yes, OWA is a self-hosted PHP application installed on your own server.

faqs.question: Does OWA support heatmaps?
faqs.answer: Yes, OWA includes a heatmap and session-recording feature called Domstream.

faqs.question: Can OWA track e-commerce?
faqs.answer: Yes, OWA supports tracking e-commerce transactions alongside pageview and visitor data.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious website analytics
use_cases.description: Website owners who want to avoid sending visitor data to third-party analytics services can self-host OWA on their own infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Multi-site agencies
use_cases.description: Manage analytics for multiple websites from a single OWA instance instead of separate tracking accounts per site.
use_cases.audience: Agencies and developers managing multiple sites

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Open Web Analytics?
paragraphs: Open Web Analytics (OWA) is a free, open-source web analytics framework written in PHP, positioned as an alternative to commercial tools like Google Analytics. It is self-hosted, so the data collected on visits, pageviews, and e-commerce transactions stays on the operator's own server.

Beyond standard traffic reporting, OWA includes heatmap and session-recording tooling (Domstream), visitor geolocation, a REST API, and an extensible module framework, letting developers instrument tracking and build custom reports on top of the raw data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Open Web Analytics is for
paragraphs: OWA suits developers and website owners who want full control over their analytics data and are comfortable self-hosting a PHP/MySQL application, particularly those who value heatmaps and session recording without paying for a separate tool.

enrichment.short_description: Ackee is a free, open-source, self-hosted Node.js analytics tool built for privacy, with no cookies and no unique user tracking.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ackee: self-hosted, open-source Node.js website analytics with a GraphQL API and cookie-free, privacy-first tracking. MIT licensed.

features.title: Privacy-first tracking
features.description: No cookies, no unique visitor identifiers, and a multi-step anonymization process.

features.title: GraphQL API
features.description: Fully documented GraphQL API for querying stats and building custom dashboards or integrations.

features.title: Multi-domain dashboard
features.description: Monitor unlimited websites from one Ackee instance.

features.title: Custom event tracking
features.description: Track clicks, form submissions, and other custom interactions.

features.title: Core traffic metrics
features.description: Reports views, referrers, visit duration, and device/OS/browser breakdown.

features.title: Flexible deployment
features.description: Deploy via Docker, Heroku, Netlify, Vercel, or manual installation.

pros: Free and open source under the MIT license
No cookies or cookie-consent banner required
GraphQL API enables flexible custom integrations
Minimal, focused dashboard UI
Multiple ready-made deployment paths (Docker, Heroku, Netlify, Vercel)
cons: Requires self-hosting Node.js and MongoDB infrastructure
No official managed or cloud-hosted version
Feature set is more basic than full-featured analytics suites
No built-in e-commerce or funnel tracking documented

faqs.question: Is Ackee free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ackee is free, open-source software under the MIT license.

faqs.question: Does Ackee use cookies?
faqs.answer: No, Ackee is designed to work without cookies and does not uniquely track individual users.

faqs.question: What does Ackee run on?
faqs.answer: Ackee is a Node.js application that stores data in MongoDB, deployable via Docker, Heroku, Netlify, or Vercel.

faqs.question: Can I access Ackee's data programmatically?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ackee exposes a fully documented GraphQL API.

use_cases.title: Privacy-compliant site tracking
use_cases.description: Developers who need GDPR-friendly analytics without cookie consent banners.

use_cases.title: Custom analytics dashboards
use_cases.description: Teams building bespoke reporting tools on top of Ackee's GraphQL API.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Ackee?
paragraphs: Ackee is a self-hosted, Node.js-based web analytics tool built around privacy. It runs on a developer's own server with MongoDB for storage, and it avoids cookies and unique user identifiers, instead using a multi-step anonymization process to report aggregate traffic metrics.

The dashboard covers core metrics like views, referrers, visit duration, and device details, and Ackee also supports custom event tracking and a fully documented GraphQL API for teams that want to build their own reporting layer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Ackee is for
paragraphs: Ackee fits developers and site owners who want a lightweight, privacy-first alternative to mainstream analytics platforms and are willing to self-host a Node.js/MongoDB service.

enrichment.short_description: Counter.dev is a free, open-source, privacy-friendly web analytics service with no cookies, logging, or IP fingerprinting.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Counter.dev: simple, open-source web analytics with no cookies, logging, or IP fingerprinting. Pay-what-you-want, AGPL-3.0 licensed.

features.title: Cookie-free tracking
features.description: No cookies, no logging, and no IP address fingerprinting.

features.title: Unique visitor counts
features.description: Reports unique daily visitor numbers.

features.title: Referral source tracking
features.description: Shows which sites are linking to your pages.

features.title: Geographic reporting
features.description: Visualizes visitor location data.

features.title: Simplified dashboard
features.description: Single-page dashboard showing only essential traffic metrics.

features.title: Open-source codebase
features.description: Source published on GitHub under the AGPL-3.0 license.

pros: Free to use, with an optional pay-what-you-want model
Strong privacy stance: no cookies or IP fingerprinting
Open source under AGPL-3.0
Very simple, distraction-free dashboard
cons: Minimal feature set compared to full analytics platforms (no heatmaps, funnels, or custom events documented)
No documented official self-hosting instructions on the marketing site
No advanced segmentation documented

faqs.question: Is Counter.dev free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Counter.dev is free to use, with an optional 'pay when ready' payment model rather than a mandatory subscription.

faqs.question: Does Counter.dev use cookies?
faqs.answer: No, Counter.dev tracks visitors without cookies, logging, or IP address fingerprinting.

faqs.question: Is Counter.dev open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, its code is published on GitHub under the AGPL-3.0 license.

use_cases.title: Lightweight analytics for personal sites
use_cases.description: Bloggers and small site owners who want basic traffic numbers without complex setup.

use_cases.title: Privacy-first analytics
use_cases.description: Teams that want to avoid cookie consent banners while still tracking visits and referrers.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Counter?
paragraphs: Counter (counter.dev) is a free, open-source web analytics service built around simplicity and privacy. It reports unique daily visitors, referral sources, and visitor geography without using cookies, server logging, or IP address fingerprinting.

The service uses a 'pay when ready' model: it can be used for free indefinitely, with payment left as an optional, user-initiated choice rather than a required subscription. Its source code is published on GitHub under the AGPL-3.0 license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Counter is for
paragraphs: Counter fits bloggers, indie developers, and small site owners who want a no-fuss traffic dashboard and prefer a privacy-respecting alternative to cookie-based analytics.

enrichment.short_description: Flagsmith is an open-source feature flag and remote config platform for web, mobile, and server apps, deployable in the cloud or self-hosted.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Flagsmith: open-source feature flags, remote config, and A/B testing with 13+ SDKs. Free plan available; paid plans start at $40/month.

features.title: Feature flags
features.description: Toggle features across web, mobile, and server-side applications with 13+ official SDKs.

features.title: Remote configuration
features.description: Change application behavior in real time without redeploying code.

features.title: Segmentation & targeting
features.description: Target rollouts by environment, user traits/segments, or percentage.

features.title: A/B & multivariate testing
features.description: Run percentage-split experiments and variations.

features.title: Change tracking
features.description: Audit logs and rollback capabilities for flag changes.

features.title: Flexible deployment
features.description: SaaS cloud across 8 regions, private cloud, or self-hosted via Docker, Kubernetes (Helm), or OpenShift.

pros: Open-source core (BSD-3-Clause) with 6.5k+ GitHub stars
Free plan covers unlimited flags, environments, and segments up to 50,000 requests/month
Deployment flexibility: cloud, private cloud, or fully self-hosted
13+ official SDKs across major languages and frameworks
SOC 2 compliant with enterprise features (SSO, RBAC, audit logs) on higher tiers
cons: Free plan is limited to 1 team member and 50,000 requests/month
Advanced governance (SSO, roles, audit logs) requires the Scale-Up tier or above
Enterprise pricing is quote-based, not published
Self-hosting requires managing your own Docker/Kubernetes infrastructure

faqs.question: Is Flagsmith open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, Flagsmith's core platform is open source under the BSD-3-Clause license and available on GitHub.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Flagsmith?
faqs.answer: Yes, Flagsmith supports self-hosted deployment via Docker, Kubernetes (Helm), or OpenShift, in addition to managed cloud and private cloud options.

faqs.question: Does Flagsmith have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Free plan includes unlimited feature flags, environments, and segments for 1 team member with up to 50,000 API requests per month.

faqs.question: What languages does Flagsmith support?
faqs.answer: Flagsmith offers 13+ SDKs, including REST, React, Next.js, Node, Android/Kotlin, iOS, Flutter, Python, Ruby, .NET, PHP, Go, and Rust.

use_cases.title: Gradual feature rollouts
use_cases.description: Engineering teams that want to release features to a percentage of users and roll back instantly if issues arise.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted flag management
use_cases.description: Organizations with data residency or compliance requirements that need to run feature flags on their own infrastructure.

use_cases.title: A/B testing
use_cases.description: Teams running percentage-split experiments to validate feature changes before full rollout.

features: Unlimited feature flags, environments, and segments
Roles, permissions, change requests, audit logs
Cloud, private cloud, or self-hosted deployment

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Flagsmith?
paragraphs: Flagsmith is an open-source feature flag and remote configuration platform that lets engineering teams control feature releases, run A/B and multivariate tests, and change application behavior without redeploying code, across web, mobile, and server-side apps.

The platform can be used as a managed SaaS across 8 cloud regions, as a private cloud instance, or fully self-hosted via Docker, Kubernetes, or OpenShift. Its core is open source under the BSD-3-Clause license, with paid tiers adding higher usage limits, SSO, and governance controls.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Flagsmith is for
paragraphs: Flagsmith suits engineering and product teams that want feature flag infrastructure with deployment flexibility, from small teams on the free plan to regulated enterprises that need self-hosted or private-cloud deployment.

enrichment.short_description: Unleash is an open-source feature management platform for gradual rollouts, kill switches, and A/B testing, self-hosted or via managed cloud.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Unleash: open-source feature flags with gradual rollouts, kill switches, and 15+ SDKs. Free self-hosted edition; paid plans from $75/seat/month.

features.title: Runtime flag evaluation
features.description: Evaluates flags locally at runtime with no added network latency.

features.title: Gradual rollouts
features.description: Canary releases and progressive delivery to subsets of users.

features.title: Kill switches
features.description: Instantly disable features during incidents.

features.title: A/B testing
features.description: Built-in experimentation support.

features.title: Governance controls
features.description: Role-based access control, audit logs, and change request workflows.

features.title: Broad SDK support
features.description: 15+ official SDKs, including Node.js, Java, Go, Python, .NET, Ruby, PHP, Rust, React, and Vue.

pros: Open source under AGPL-3.0-or-later with 13.7k+ GitHub stars
Free, self-hosted open-source edition available via Docker
SOC 2 Type II certified and GDPR compliant
SSO support (SAML 2.0, OpenID Connect, Okta, Azure AD) on paid tiers
Large SDK ecosystem covering 15+ languages and frameworks
cons: Paid Pay-as-you-go tier starts at $75/seat/month with a 5-seat minimum for self-hosted deployments
Enterprise pricing is custom/quote-based
Advanced governance and compliance features require a paid plan
Self-hosted open-source edition requires managing your own infrastructure

faqs.question: Is Unleash open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, Unleash's core is open source under the AGPL-3.0-or-later license and available on GitHub with 13.7k+ stars.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Unleash for free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the open-source edition can be self-hosted for free via Docker.

faqs.question: What does the paid plan cost?
faqs.answer: Unleash's Pay-as-you-go plan starts at $75/seat/month, with a 14-day free trial and no credit card required to start.

faqs.question: Does Unleash support SSO?
faqs.answer: Yes, SAML 2.0, OpenID Connect, Okta, and Azure AD SSO are supported on paid and enterprise plans.

use_cases.title: Enterprise progressive delivery
use_cases.description: Large organizations rolling out features gradually with instant rollback and kill switches for incident response.

use_cases.title: Free self-hosted flag management
use_cases.description: Teams that want full control over feature flag infrastructure without per-seat costs.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Unleash?
paragraphs: Unleash is an open-source feature management platform that lets teams decouple deployments from releases using feature flags. It supports gradual rollouts, canary releases, A/B testing, and kill switches for rapid incident response, evaluating flags at runtime with no added latency.

The open-source edition is free and self-hostable via Docker, while paid Pay-as-you-go and Enterprise tiers add SSO, RBAC, audit logs, and managed cloud or hybrid deployment options with SOC 2 Type II and GDPR compliance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Unleash is for
paragraphs: Unleash fits engineering teams ranging from cost-conscious teams self-hosting the free open-source edition to larger enterprises that need SSO, compliance certifications, and dedicated support on paid plans.

enrichment.short_description: Flipt is a Git-native, self-hosted feature flag platform where flag changes become reviewable Git commits, deployable as a single binary.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Flipt: Git-native feature flags with single-binary self-hosted deployment. Free open-source tier; Pro plan starts at $200/month.

features.title: Git-native flag storage
features.description: UI changes to flags generate reviewable Git commits with full version history.

features.title: Real-time streaming updates
features.description: Server-Sent Events (SSE) propagate flag changes instantly without polling.

features.title: Multi-environment support
features.description: Isolated environments per branch, directory, or repository.

features.title: Single-binary deployment
features.description: Runs as one binary with zero required external dependencies by default.

features.title: Broad SDK support
features.description: SDKs for Go, Python, JavaScript, Java, C#, Swift, Kotlin, Dart, and more.

features.title: DevOps integrations
features.description: Native support for GitHub, GitLab, BitBucket, and Azure DevOps workflows.

pros: Free, self-hosted open-source tier with unlimited flags
Zero-dependency single-binary deployment simplifies self-hosting
Git-native workflow gives a full audit history via commits
Real-time flag propagation via SSE streaming
4.9k+ GitHub stars
cons: Server code is licensed under the Fair Core License (source-available), not a fully permissive OSI license
Pro plan pricing ($200/month) is fixed rather than usage-scaled
Secrets management via Vault or cloud providers requires the Pro tier
Smaller ecosystem/community than more established feature-flag tools

faqs.question: Is Flipt free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Flipt's open-source tier is free forever and includes unlimited flags and Git-native workflows.

faqs.question: What license does Flipt use?
faqs.answer: Flipt's client SDKs are MIT licensed, while the server code uses the Fair Core License (source-available, converting to MIT after a future date).

faqs.question: Can I self-host Flipt?
faqs.answer: Yes, Flipt is designed to be self-hosted as a single binary with no required external dependencies.

faqs.question: What does Flipt Pro cost?
faqs.answer: Flipt Pro is $200/month and adds PR creation, secrets management, and dedicated support.

use_cases.title: Git-based flag governance
use_cases.description: Engineering teams that want feature flag changes reviewed the same way as code, via pull requests.

use_cases.title: Lightweight self-hosted flags
use_cases.description: Teams that want a zero-dependency, single-binary feature flag server they fully control.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Flipt?
paragraphs: Flipt is a feature flag management platform built around Git as the source of truth: changes made in its UI are converted into reviewable Git commits, giving teams a full audit trail and code-review-style workflow for flag changes.

It's designed for self-hosting as a single binary with no required external dependencies, and propagates flag changes in real time via Server-Sent Events. The open-source tier is free forever, while Pro and Enterprise tiers add secrets management, RBAC, and dedicated support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Flipt is for
paragraphs: Flipt suits engineering teams already working in Git who want feature flag changes to go through the same review process as code, and who prefer a lightweight, self-hosted binary over a heavier vendor platform.

enrichment.short_description: FeatBit is an open-source feature flag and experimentation platform that self-hosts via Docker Compose, Kubernetes, or AWS.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: FeatBit: open-source feature flags with A/B testing, an MCP server for AI coding agents, and self-hosting via Docker or Kubernetes.

features.title: Feature flag lifecycle management
features.description: CLI-first workflow for creating, filtering, and organizing flags.

features.title: A/B testing
features.description: Built-in experimentation for data-driven release decisions.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Deploy via Docker Compose, Kubernetes, or AWS using Terraform.

features.title: MCP server
features.description: FeatBit MCP server integrates feature flag workflows with AI coding agents.

features.title: Multi-language SDKs
features.description: Server and client SDKs for .NET, JavaScript, React, Node.js, Java, Python, and Go.

features.title: Audit logging & integrations
features.description: Tracks flag and segment changes, with Slack, Datadog, and Grafana integrations.

pros: Open source under the MIT license with 1.9k+ GitHub stars
Full self-hosting options (Docker Compose, Kubernetes, AWS)
MCP server support for AI coding agent workflows
Multi-language SDK coverage
Multi-project and multi-environment management
cons: Smaller GitHub star count and community than more established competitors like Unleash or Flagsmith
A commercial 'Professional version' exists but its pricing is not published
No documented managed cloud offering with published pricing

faqs.question: Is FeatBit open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, FeatBit is open source under the MIT license and available on GitHub.

faqs.question: Can I self-host FeatBit?
faqs.answer: Yes, FeatBit supports self-hosting via Docker Compose, Kubernetes, or AWS using Terraform.

faqs.question: Does FeatBit support AI tooling?
faqs.answer: Yes, FeatBit offers an MCP server and Copilot-style skills for AI coding agent workflows.

faqs.question: Is FeatBit free?
faqs.answer: The open-source, self-hosted version is free; a separate commercial Professional version is also offered.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted flags with data ownership
use_cases.description: Teams that want to own their infrastructure and control feature-flag data and costs.
use_cases.audience: Engineering teams needing full data ownership

use_cases.title: AI-assisted flag management
use_cases.description: Developers using AI coding agents that need MCP-based access to feature flag workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is FeatBit?
paragraphs: FeatBit is an open-source feature flag management platform that supports flag lifecycle management, user targeting, and A/B testing, with a CLI-first workflow and a growing set of AI-oriented tools including an MCP server for AI coding agents.
It is designed for self-hosting, with deployment options covering Docker Compose, Kubernetes, and AWS via Terraform, and SDKs across .NET, JavaScript, React, Node.js, Java, Python, and Go.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who FeatBit is for
paragraphs: FeatBit fits engineering teams that want to self-host feature flag infrastructure for data ownership and cost control, including teams experimenting with AI coding agents via its MCP server.

enrichment.short_description: ConfigCat is a hosted feature flag service with a forever-free plan and 30+ open-source, MIT-licensed SDKs.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ConfigCat: feature flags and remote config with a forever-free plan and 30+ open-source SDKs. Paid plans start at $110/month.

features.title: Feature toggles
features.description: Turn features on/off after deployment without redeploying code.

features.title: User segmentation
features.description: Target users by region, email, subscription, or custom attributes.

features.title: Rollout strategies
features.description: Percentage rollouts, A/B testing, and multivariate variations.

features.title: Configuration values
features.description: Store strings, numbers, and text variables alongside boolean flags.

features.title: Audit & compliance
features.description: Mandatory notes, audit logs, and compliance tracking.

features.title: Broad SDK support
features.description: 30+ open-source SDKs, including JavaScript, React, Python, Java, Go, iOS, and Android.

features.title: Third-party integrations
features.description: Connects with Slack, GitHub Actions, Jira, Datadog, Segment, and Amplitude.

pros: Forever-free plan with unlimited seats and no credit card required
Free plan includes security features like SSO, 2FA, and SAML
30+ MIT-licensed open-source SDKs
Wide integration ecosystem
Used by large organizations including Nasdaq, Rakuten, and Heineken
cons: Core platform is closed-source/hosted; only client SDKs are open source
Paid plans range from $110 to $4,500+/month depending on scale
No published self-hosted deployment option for the core platform
Higher-tier pricing (Smart, Enterprise, Dedicated) requires a larger budget

faqs.question: Does ConfigCat have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, ConfigCat offers a 'Forever Free' plan with unlimited seats and no credit card required.

faqs.question: Are ConfigCat's SDKs open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, ConfigCat provides 30+ MIT-licensed open-source SDKs across web, mobile, desktop, and backend platforms.

faqs.question: How much does ConfigCat cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at $110/month (Pro), with Smart at $325/month, Enterprise at $900/month, and Dedicated from $4,500+/month.

faqs.question: Can I self-host ConfigCat?
faqs.answer: No self-hosted deployment option for the core platform is documented; ConfigCat is a hosted service.

use_cases.title: Budget-friendly feature flags
use_cases.description: Startups and small teams that need feature flags without cost via the forever-free plan.

use_cases.title: Enterprise-grade release management
use_cases.description: Larger organizations needing audit logs, SSO, and compliance tracking for regulated releases.

features: Additional features over the Free plan

long_form_blocks.heading: What is ConfigCat?
paragraphs: ConfigCat is a hosted feature flag and remote configuration service that lets teams turn features on or off, target users, and run rollouts without redeploying applications. It provides 30+ open-source, MIT-licensed SDKs across web, mobile, desktop, and backend platforms.

The service offers a permanently free tier with unlimited seats and no credit card required, and paid plans that add higher usage limits and enterprise features, priced from $110/month up to $4,500+/month for dedicated infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who ConfigCat is for
paragraphs: ConfigCat suits development teams of any size that want a hosted feature flag service with a genuinely free starting tier, from small startups to larger organizations needing audit logs and compliance tracking.

enrichment.short_description: DevCycle is an OpenFeature-native feature flag platform with a free tier, usage-based pricing, and AI/MCP tooling, now part of Dynatrace.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: DevCycle: OpenFeature-native feature flags with usage-based pricing, a free tier, and AI/MCP tooling. Business plan starts at $500/month.

features.title: OpenFeature-native SDKs
features.description: Open-source SDKs across 15+ platforms with no vendor lock-in.

features.title: Real-time flag updates
features.description: Flag changes take effect without restarting applications.

features.title: Gradual rollouts
features.description: Incrementally release features to user subsets with rollback capability.

features.title: A/B testing
features.description: Data-driven experimentation before full release.

features.title: Roles & permissions
features.description: Granular role-based access control.

features.title: AI-generated feature summaries & MCP server
features.description: Natural-language tools for managing feature flags via AI coding agents.

pros: Free plan with no credit card required, up to 1,000 client-side MAUs
Usage-based pricing with no per-seat charges; unlimited seats on all plans
OpenFeature-native with open-source SDKs
AI/MCP tooling for feature flag management
Backed by Dynatrace following its acquisition
cons: Business plan starts at $500/month annual (or $625/month monthly)
Overage charges apply beyond included MAU/request allowances
Now owned by Dynatrace, which could affect independent product direction
Enterprise pricing is custom/quote-based

faqs.question: Does DevCycle have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, DevCycle offers a free plan with up to 1,000 client-side MAUs and no credit card required, plus a no-signup playground.

faqs.question: What does DevCycle's Business plan cost?
faqs.answer: The Business plan is $500/month billed annually (or $625/month billed monthly), including 100,000 MAUs and 1,000,000 cloud config requests/month.

faqs.question: Is DevCycle OpenFeature-native?
faqs.answer: Yes, DevCycle describes itself as the first OpenFeature-native feature management platform, with open-source SDKs.

faqs.question: Who owns DevCycle?
faqs.answer: DevCycle is now part of Dynatrace, following an acquisition.

use_cases.title: Usage-based flag management
use_cases.description: Teams that want unlimited seats and pay based on usage rather than per-user licensing.

use_cases.title: AI-assisted feature flag workflows
use_cases.description: Developers using natural-language/MCP tools to manage flags from AI coding agents.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is DevCycle?
paragraphs: DevCycle is a feature flag management platform built on the OpenFeature standard, letting teams deploy code behind feature flags, run gradual rollouts and A/B tests, and manage releases across web, backend, and mobile with 15+ SDKs and no vendor lock-in.

Pricing is usage-based rather than seat-based, with a free tier covering up to 1,000 client-side MAUs and a Business plan starting at $500/month for larger usage allowances. DevCycle is now part of Dynatrace following its acquisition, while continuing to operate under the DevCycle name.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who DevCycle is for
paragraphs: DevCycle suits engineering teams that want OpenFeature-compliant, vendor-neutral feature flag tooling with usage-based pricing rather than per-seat licensing, including teams experimenting with AI-assisted flag management.

enrichment.short_description: MariaDB Server is a free, open-source relational database, created by MySQL's original developers as a drop-in MySQL replacement.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MariaDB Server: free, open-source GPLv2 relational database and drop-in MySQL replacement, used by Wikipedia, WordPress.com, and Google.

features.title: Galera Cluster 4
features.description: Synchronous multi-master clustering for high availability.

features.title: Oracle Database compatibility
features.description: Compatibility features that ease migration from Oracle Database.

features.title: Temporal tables
features.description: System-versioned tables for querying historical data.

features.title: Pluggable storage engines
features.description: Extensible storage-engine architecture.

features.title: Rich data types
features.description: Support for JSON, BLOB, TEXT, and GEOMETRY data types.

features.title: Online schema change
features.description: Modify schema without locking tables.

pros: Free and open source under GPLv2, guaranteed to remain so by the MariaDB Foundation
Drop-in replacement for MySQL, easing migration
Default database in most major Linux distributions and available on most cloud platforms
Used in production by Wikipedia, WordPress.com, and Google
Commercial enterprise support and hardened builds available via MariaDB Corporation for organizations that need them
cons: Enterprise features (Enterprise Server, Enterprise Cluster, MaxScale) require a separate commercial contract with MariaDB Corporation
Some newer MySQL features and compatibility can lag or diverge over time
Self-managed deployment requires in-house database administration expertise

faqs.question: Is MariaDB free?
faqs.answer: Yes, MariaDB Server is free and open source under the GPLv2 license, maintained by the non-profit MariaDB Foundation.

faqs.question: Is MariaDB compatible with MySQL?
faqs.answer: Yes, MariaDB was created as a drop-in replacement for MySQL by MySQL's original developers, including Monty Widenius.

faqs.question: When was MariaDB created?
faqs.answer: MariaDB Server was created in 2009, after Sun Microsystems acquired MySQL AB.

faqs.question: Is there a paid version of MariaDB?
faqs.answer: The core MariaDB Server is free; MariaDB Corporation separately offers commercial Enterprise Server, clustering, and support contracts.

use_cases.title: Open-source MySQL replacement
use_cases.description: Organizations migrating off MySQL who want a compatible, community-governed, open-source database.

use_cases.title: High-availability clustering
use_cases.description: Teams needing synchronous multi-master replication via Galera Cluster for high availability.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is MariaDB?
paragraphs: MariaDB Server is a free, open-source relational database management system created in 2009 by the original developers of MySQL, following Sun Microsystems' acquisition of MySQL AB. It is designed as a drop-in replacement for MySQL and is licensed under GPLv2.

The database is maintained by the non-profit MariaDB Foundation, which is incorporated in Delaware, USA, and it is the default database in most major Linux distributions and widely available on cloud platforms. Notable users include Wikipedia, WordPress.com, and Google. A separate commercial entity, MariaDB Corporation, offers enterprise-hardened builds, clustering tools, and paid support contracts on top of the free open-source core.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who MariaDB is for
paragraphs: MariaDB fits organizations that want a free, open-source, MySQL-compatible relational database with strong community governance, from individual developers self-hosting a single instance to enterprises running Galera Cluster for high availability.
